description: This chart is a scatter plot illustrating the relationship between aluminium (Al) content (x-axis, wt.%) and mass density (left y-axis, g/cm³) as well as relative density reduction (right y-axis, %) for Fe-Mn-Al-C low-density austenitic steels. Conventional TRIP/TWIP steels are represented by a circular data point at ~3 wt.% Al with a density of ~7.28 g/cm³, while square data points correspond to low-density steel variants including TRIPLEX steels, low-density TWIP steels and low-density duplex steels, grouped by manganese (Mn) content ranges: <18 wt.% Mn, 18–26 wt.% Mn, and 26–30 wt.% Mn. A reference mark indicates the density of conventional steels is 7.874 g/cm³. A clear negative linear trend is observed: as Al content increases, steel mass density decreases, reaching ~6.4 g/cm³ at ~12.7 wt.% Al, corresponding to a maximum 18% density reduction. The figure supports the conclusion that Al alloying is an effective strategy to reduce the density of Fe-Mn-Al-C steels while retaining the beneficial properties of TWIP/TRIP steels for lightweight applications.

This plot displays the specific energy absorption (E_spec, unit: J/mm³) of Fe-Mn-Al-C alloys as a function of aluminium content (ranging from 6 to 11 wt.%). Three conventional deep-drawing steels, including DC04 - FeP04, HC300LA - ZStE300, and high strength IF steel, are included as benchmarks, all with E_spec values below 0.2 J/mm³. Fe-Mn-Al-C alloys are tested at two deformation rates: 10⁻⁴ s⁻¹ and 10⁻³ s⁻¹. Their E_spec generally increases with rising Al content, varying from ~0.26 J/mm³ at ~6.5 wt.% Al up to ~0.55 J/mm³ at ~11 wt.% Al, which is far higher than the reference conventional steels. This figure supports the core conclusion that Fe-Mn-Al-C austenitic steels have outstanding energy absorption performance, making them excellent low-density high-performance structural material candidates.

This group of plots presents mechanical properties of Fe-30Mn-1.2C-xAl (x=0, 2, 4, 6, 8 wt.%) austenitic steels under as-homogenised condition and different aging treatments (450°C/1h, 500°C/1h/24h, 550°C/1h/24h, 600°C/1h/24h) from Rapid Alloy Prototyping experiments. Subplot (a) shows yield stress (YS, unit MPa), (b) shows ultimate tensile stress (UTS, unit MPa), (c) shows total elongation (TE, unit %), (d) shows hardness (unit HBW). Key trends: as Al content increases to 8 wt.%, YS, UTS and hardness of aged samples increase drastically, e.g., 550°C 24h aged 8Al sample reaches ~940 MPa YS, ~1020 MPa UTS, ~360 HBW, while TE drops significantly (as low as ~7% for 600°C 24h aged 8Al sample). This figure demonstrates that increasing Al content and appropriate aging treatment greatly improve strength of the steels via κ-carbide precipitation at the cost of ductility, supporting microstructure-property correlation for low-density Fe-Mn-Al-C steel design.

This plot presents tensile strength (y-axis, unit: MPa) versus elongation to fracture (x-axis, unit: %) for Fe-30Mn-1.2C-xAl low-density austenitic steels, with Al content of 0, 2, 4, 6, 8 wt% distinguished by different colored markers as per the legend. The samples undergo tempering treatments at 450, 500, 550, 600°C for 1 h (denoted T) or 24 h (denoted T+t). Key trends are observed: 0Al alloy exhibits high ductility up to ~75% elongation with ~800 MPa tensile strength, while aging reduces its strength and ductility. The 8Al alloy shows the opposite trend: 24 h aging increases its tensile strength to ~970 MPa but reduces elongation to ~25%, while unaged 8Al samples have comparable performance to 0Al samples. Intermediate Al content (2,4,6 wt%) alloys show mechanical properties between these two extremes, with 4 and 6 wt% Al alloys only weakly affected by applied aging treatments. This figure supports that Al content and aging parameters (time, temperature) control the mechanical performance of this steel series, with high Al content leading to age hardening attributed to nanosized κ-carbide precipitation, while low Al content alloys suffer embrittlement after prolonged aging.

This figure presents combined atom probe tomography (APT) and transmission electron microscopy (TEM) characterization of κ-carbides in Fe-30Mn-1.2C-8Al low-density austenitic steel aged at 600 °C for 24 h. The first subfigure is APT 3D reconstruction: purple iso-surfaces (9 at.% C) represent L'12 structured κ-carbides, with cyan, magenta, gold dots marking C, Mn, Al atoms respectively, and [001], [100] crystallographic orientations labelled. The second subfigure shows element concentration profiles along the 100 nm-length APT analysis cylinder, revealing alternating austenite (γ) and κ-carbide regions: Fe concentration is higher in γ matrix, while Mn, Al, C are obviously enriched in κ-carbides. The third subfigure is bright-field TEM image with 20 nm scale bar, displaying uniformly distributed nanosized cuboidal/rod-shaped κ-carbides (~20 nm size) in austenite matrix, matching APT results. This figure validates the formation of thermally stable nano κ-carbide precipitates during aging, which serve as key strengthening phases for the alloy.

This figure presents combinatorial screening results for Fe-20Mn-0.4C-xAl (wt.%) low-density TWIP steels. The first four subplots illustrate mechanical property trends as functions of Al content (0 to ~11 wt.%) under four treatment conditions: as-quenched, 400°C aging for 24h, 500°C aging for 24h, and 600°C aging for 24h, covering yield strength (MPa), ultimate tensile strength (UTS, MPa), tensile elongation (%), and Brinell hardness (HBW). The remaining four stacked bar charts show phase volume fractions (γ austenite, ε martensite, α ferrite, κ carbide) for alloys with 0, 2, 8, 11 wt.% Al after tempering at 400°C, 500°C, 600°C. Key trends: increasing Al content improves yield strength, UTS and hardness, while reducing tensile elongation between 2 wt.% and 11 wt.% Al. Low-Al (0, 2 wt.%) alloys remain nearly single-phase austenite, α ferrite forms when Al ≥8 wt.%, and κ carbide appears at 11 wt.% Al after 600°C tempering. The core conclusion is that the observed mechanical property evolution is mainly driven by rising α ferrite content with increasing Al, rather than ε martensite or κ carbide formation.

This figure presents true stress (unit: GPa) versus true strain curves for low-density Fe-Mn-Al-C austenitic TWIP steels, tested at seven temperatures: 293K, 373K, 473K, 573K, 673K, 773K, and 873K. For each temperature, solid lines represent simulation results from a dislocation density-based constitutive strain hardening model, while cross markers correspond to experimental tensile test measurements. A clear trend is observed: flow stress at the same true strain decreases monotonically with increasing test temperature, e.g., true stress at 0.5 true strain drops from ~1.35 GPa at 293K to ~0.46 GPa at 873K. The simulated curves show excellent agreement with experimental data across all tested temperatures and strain ranges, which validates the accuracy of the applied strain hardening model for predicting the temperature-dependent mechanical behavior of this class of low-density austenitic steels.

This figure presents the strain hardening (vertical axis, unit GPa) versus true strain (horizontal axis, range 0 to 0.5) of Fe-22wt.% Mn-0.6wt.% C model TWIP steel at seven different temperatures: 293K, 373K, 473K, 573K, 673K, 773K, 873K. Solid lines correspond to simulation results from a dislocation density-based constitutive strain hardening model that uses a single coherent parameter set for the entire temperature range, while cross markers are experimental measurement data points. A clear decreasing trend of strain hardening capacity with rising temperature is observed: higher temperature leads to lower initial peak hardening value and lower overall strain hardening level across the full strain range. The simulation curves show good agreement with experimental data, which validates that the adopted strain hardening model can reliably predict the mechanical behavior of low stacking fault energy fcc alloys over a wide temperature range using one unified parameter set.

This figure characterizes hydrogen-induced cracking micromechanisms in κ-carbide strengthened low-density Fe-26Mn-11Al-1.2C austenitic steel, combining EBSD and ECCI characterization. Subfigure (a) is EBSD-derived longitudinal direction inverse pole figure (RD-IPF) orientation mapping with a 50 μm scale bar, showing grain orientation distribution around the intergranular crack (black regions). Subfigure (b) is Kernel average misorientation (KAM) map calculated from EBSD orientation gradients (first neighboring point shell, 300 nm spacing, KAM value range 0 to 5) with a 50 μm scale bar, revealing high strain localization (high KAM, yellow/red regions) surrounding the crack. Subfigure (c) is orientation-optimized electron channeling contrast imaging (ECCI) of the white dashed box region in (b) with a 10 μm scale bar, showing slip localization in adjacent grains and microvoids nucleated along grain boundaries. The figure supports that hydrogen embrittlement in this alloy is partially induced by κ-carbide-promoted planar slip localization, which leads to microvoid coalescence and intergranular cracking.

This figure presents tensile test results of low-density Fe-Mn-Al-C austenitic steels with and without hydrogen charging, as well as supporting thermal desorption analysis data. The main plot shows engineering stress (MPa) versus engineering plastic strain (%): specimens without hydrogen charging exhibit an ultimate tensile strength around 1000 MPa and total elongation over 40%, while hydrogen-charged specimens show significantly reduced elongation, indicating obvious hydrogen-induced ductility loss. The inset is the thermal desorption analysis curve obtained at a heating rate of 26 K/min, plotting hydrogen desorption rate (wt ppm/s) against temperature (°C), including experimental data, peak fit curves, and cumulative fit results, with two distinct desorption peaks corresponding to hydrogen trap sites (κ-carbides as identified). This figure supports that hydrogen charging deteriorates the ductility of the studied steel, and κ-carbides act as hydrogen trap sites related to its hydrogen embrittlement susceptibility.
